mnemonics.title: HADOOP HISTORY TIMELINE
mnemonics.content: LUCENE IN 1999, NUTCH STARTS 2002, GFS 2003, MAPREDUCE 2004, HADOOP 2006, TOP-LEVEL 2008, 1.0 IN 2011, YARN IN 2013, 3.0 IN 2017 - REMEMBER: LNN-GMM-H1Y3 (Lucene Nutch Nutch-Start GFS MapReduce Hadoop-Start Year)

acronyms.usage: The precursor to HDFS developed in the Nutch project in 2004
The distributed file system that is the storage layer of Hadoop
The resource management component introduced in Hadoop 2.0
The Google paper from 2003 that inspired HDFS design
The programming model inspired by Google's 2004 paper

visualTips.visualization: Draw a horizontal timeline from 1999 to 2017. Place Lucene at 1999, Nutch at 2002, GFS at 2003, MapReduce at 2004, Hadoop birth at 2006, Apache TLP at 2008, Hadoop 1.0 at 2011, YARN at 2013, and Hadoop 3.0 at 2017. The timeline shows evolution from web search (Lucene/Nutch) to Big Data platform.

Think of a pyramid: Bottom layer is the PROBLEM (web data explosion), middle layer is the INSPIRATION (Google papers GFS & MapReduce), top layer is the SOLUTION (Hadoop). This shows how practical problems + research insights = revolutionary technology.

Draw a family tree: Grandparent = Lucene (1999), Parent = Nutch (2002), Children = NDFS + MapReduce (2004), Grandchild = Hadoop (2006), Great-grandchildren = HBase, Hive, Pig, Spark, etc. This shows the genealogy of Hadoop ecosystem.

analogies.analogy: Just as a specialized tool developed for one purpose (cutting wood) evolves into a general-purpose tool (Swiss army knife), the Nutch project created distributed computing tools that evolved into the general-purpose Hadoop framework.
Google's GFS and MapReduce papers were like architectural blueprints that Doug Cutting used to build Hadoop, similar to how an engineer uses published research to build a bridge.
Yahoo was to Hadoop what a test kitchen is to a new recipe - they took the initial idea and refined it into something production-ready that could handle real-world demands at scale.
Naming Hadoop after a toy elephant is like naming a powerful technology after a child's toy - it shows how innovation often comes from personal, humble origins rather than corporate boardrooms.
